title.default: Deralis Digital: I build the systems businesses need to operate

metadata.description: Most businesses already have tools. What they are missing is a system. I build the system that fixes that.

footer.tagline: I build the systems businesses need to operate.
footer.location: Île-de-France · Remote for France, the UK, and the US

auditCta.headline: Nothing is built until we agree it's worth building.
auditCta.body1: The Systems Audit maps your current operation, identifies structural gaps, and defines what to build and in what order.
auditCta.body2: If the project proceeds, the audit fee is deducted from the build.
auditCta.note: Fixed price. No commitment beyond the audit itself.

hero.headline: Tools, messages, files, spreadsheets. Everything works, but nothing is really connected.
hero.sub1: What you're using today to run your business doesn't hold up.
hero.sub2: I build the system that replaces it, for businesses with more tools than clarity. Information goes where it needs to, when it needs to, without waiting on someone to move it forward.

anchor.backgroundProse: Four years of technical client work across French software and industrial companies. Eighteen months inside the equipment rental industry at Loxam, where the domain knowledge behind TraviXO came from.
anchor.built1Desc: Multi-tenant SaaS for VGP compliance in French equipment rental. Built solo. Live.
anchor.built2Desc: Passport intake MVP designed and pitched to a Paris consular office.
anchor.signature: Independent. Bilingual French and English. One person, every line of code.

who.title: Established businesses where the operation has outgrown the tools holding it together.
who.intro: The person who can decide to fix this is in the room. If that's not you, this is the wrong time.

card1.title: Teams running operations manually
card1.description: You rely on people to keep things working day-to-day. The process exists in someone's head, not in a system.

card2.title: Businesses outgrowing their tools
card2.description: Spreadsheets, SaaS tools, and workarounds are no longer enough. Information lives in multiple places and moves inconsistently.

card3.title: Not for everyone
card3.description: Not for early-stage ideas, basic website requests, or projects where the problem has not been defined. If you cannot name what is broken, the timing is wrong.

casestudy.label: Case study
casestudy.title: TraviXO Systems
casestudy.context: B2B SaaS · Equipment rental · VGP compliance
casestudy.prose: Multi-tenant SaaS for VGP compliance in French equipment rental. Built solo. Live. Shaped by real exposure to how these operations run.

howIWork.title: A sequential process, not a menu of options.
howIWork.intro: Each stage exists because the one before it earned it. Nothing is assumed and nothing is skipped.

1.title: Systems Audit
1.description: I map how your operation actually runs today and define what to build and in what order.

2.title: Custom System
2.description: The core build. One system that shows where work is, what is blocked, and what needs to happen next.

3.title: Extensions
3.description: Automation, integrations, and additional workflows built on top of the working foundation.

4.title: Ongoing
4.description: Maintenance and iteration for clients who want continued support. No lock-in, no hidden retainers.

finalCta.headline: If something is breaking, or not working as it should, I can show you how to fix it.
finalCta.description: Before anything is priced, I map how your system actually works. Then we decide together if it's worth building.
finalCta.note: Another developer can take over without rebuilding everything.

hero.headline: The work I do, in the order I do it.
hero.description: Every engagement starts by mapping how your operation actually runs. From there, the build follows a sequence where each step earns the next. No fixed packages, no upfront quotes, no work without a written brief.

coreServices.title: Three sequential stages. Each one earns the next.

1.name: Systems Audit

meta.whoValue: Businesses experiencing friction in their operations
meta.timelineValue: One session, written brief delivered after

1.prose1: Before anything is built, I map how your operation actually works today. We walk through the tools you use, the people involved, where information lives, where it gets stuck, and where the friction is costing you time or money.
1.prose2: The audit produces a written brief that defines what to build, why it matters, and in what order. If the brief identifies a project worth doing, the €300 is deducted from the build cost. If it identifies that nothing needs to be built right now, I will tell you that directly.

2.name: Custom System

meta.whoValue: Teams running operations manually or across fragmented tools
meta.timelineValue: Defined after the audit, based on real scope

2.prose1: The core build. One system that shows where work is, what is blocked, and what needs to happen next. Designed around the specific operation the audit mapped, not around a template.
2.prose2: This usually replaces a combination of spreadsheets, disconnected SaaS tools, and informal processes that live in someone’s head. The goal is a single source of truth the team actually uses, not a dashboard that gets ignored after launch.
2.prose3: Custom System work is always scoped against the audit brief. The brief defines what gets built, what does not, and what comes later as Extensions.

3.name: Extensions

meta.whoValue: Clients with a working system who need additional capabilities
meta.timelineValue: Defined per extension, smaller than core build

3.prose1: Extensions are automation, integrations, and additional workflows built on top of a working foundation. Extensions only happen after a Custom System exists and is being used. Building extensions on top of a system that does not yet work creates more problems than it solves.
3.prose2: Common extensions include connecting the system to existing tools the team already uses, automating recurring tasks that currently happen by hand, and adding reporting or visibility layers for stakeholders who need to see the data without operating it directly.
3.prose3: Extensions are always defined against the original audit brief, with each extension scoped individually.

ongoing.title: Ongoing Support
ongoing.context: Optional. Available after a Custom System is delivered.
ongoing.prose1: Once a system is live, it needs maintenance, small iterations, and the occasional fix. Ongoing Support is the post-delivery layer for clients who want continued support without the friction of a new contract every time something needs attention.
ongoing.prose2: This covers updates, performance monitoring, bug fixes, system documentation, and on-demand support. New features and significant additions are scoped separately as Extensions.
ongoing.prose3: No lock-in, no minimum term. Cancel any month.
ongoing.priceNote: Available only after a Custom System has been built.

questions.title: Questions clients ask before booking the audit.

1.question: How much does a typical project cost?
1.answer: Every system is different and the cost depends on what your operation actually needs, not on a fixed package. Before quoting anything, I run a Systems Audit to map your operation and define what needs to be built. The audit costs €300 and produces a written brief. If the project proceeds, the €300 is deducted from the build cost.

2.question: Do you work with clients outside France?
2.answer: Yes. I work remotely with clients across France, the UK, and the US. Communication is in French or English depending on what works for you. Audit sessions and project work happen over video calls, with the deliverables and the system itself accessible from anywhere.

3.question: Can you take over an existing project?
3.answer: Sometimes. The Systems Audit covers existing systems too, not just operations that have never been digitized. If the existing system is salvageable, the brief will define what to keep, what to rebuild, and what to deprecate. If it is not salvageable, the brief will say so, and you can decide whether to start over or keep what you have.

4.question: How long does a typical project take?
4.answer: Defined after the audit. The brief includes a realistic timeline for the work it scopes, based on the complexity of your operation and the depth of the build. Projects can range from a few weeks for a focused Custom System to several months for a deeper engagement with multiple Extensions. The audit is the only stage with a fixed timeline: one session, brief delivered shortly after.

finalCta.headline: Ready to map how your system actually works?
finalCta.description: The audit is the only thing you can buy upfront. Everything else gets defined here first.
finalCta.note: Another developer can take over without rebuilding everything.

I built the digital flow that would have closed the gap, and proposed it directly after meeting in person. The proposal is currently with them.

metadata.title: Projects · Deralis Digital
metadata.description: One live system, one spec pitch, and a set of working concept builds. The work as it actually stands today, with no fabricated metrics or invented client results.

hero.title: Real systems, built around how operations actually run.
hero.sub1: One live system, one spec pitch in front of a real buyer, and a small set of working concept builds.
hero.sub2: Each one started from the same observation. Most businesses already have tools. They just do not have a system.
hero.sub3: No fabricated metrics. No client logos I have not earned.

travixo.label: Case study
travixo.context: B2B software · Equipment rental · VGP compliance · Live
travixo.prose1: TraviXO is compliance software built for the French equipment rental industry. The system gives teams one place to track equipment, manage compliance, and see what needs attention next.
travixo.prose2: Time inside the equipment rental industry at Loxam put me close to how these operations actually run. The deeper picture of how VGP compliance fails came from studying the regulation and from conversations with rental teams.
travixo.prose3: Over time, the same failure points kept surfacing. Inspections get missed. Certificates end up scattered across emails or folders. The audit trail depends on someone remembering to keep it updated. The system was built around removing those failure points.
travixo.prose4: Built solo. Live. Designed as a working operational system, not a concept.

govPortal.label: Spec pitch
govPortal.title: Government Application Portal
govPortal.context: Public sector · Passport intake MVP · Currently with the buyer
govPortal.prose1: A passport application intake MVP I built and pitched to a Paris consular office after going through their process myself. Their existing flow required an in-person trip for files I had already uploaded online. The gap between what the system could do and what the team was asked to do was visible from the waiting room.
govPortal.prose2: I built the digital flow that would have closed the gap and proposed it directly. The proposal is currently with them.
govPortal.note: Spec pitch. Not a delivered engagement. Status updated when the buyer responds.

screenshot.alt: Government Application Portal intake flow showing document submission and status tracking

concepts.title: Working demos you can click.
concepts.intro: Not client work. Working demos built to test patterns before they are used inside a real system. Each one is live, public, and runs on real code rather than staged screens.

1.title: Deralis Ops Dashboard
1.desc: An operations dashboard pattern for teams that need to see where work actually is. Built to test the visibility primitive a real client system would extend.

2.title: Flux Client
2.desc: A structured client intake demo. An inquiry arrives, becomes a job record, gets a status, and stays visible to a whole team without anyone forwarding anything.

3.title: Onboard
3.desc: A structured onboarding tracker. Create a record, watch the checklist appear, every step visible. Nothing depending on someone remembering.

4.title: Marche Diaspora
4.desc: A product catalogue with order management. End-to-end ordering flow as an interactive demo, built to test how a small commerce operation could run on one system.

finalCta.title: Every project here started as a problem someone could name.
finalCta.body: The trigger is usually the same. A loss of control, visibility, or flow that the existing tools were never going to fix on their own. The systems audit is where that conversation starts.
finalCta.note: Fixed price. Bilingual French and English. One person, every line of code.

metadata.title: About: Independent Engineering Practice
metadata.description: I build the systems established businesses need to keep working as they grow. Not websites. Not templates. Systems that show where work is, what is blocked, and what needs to happen next.

hero.title: About
hero.description: I build the systems established businesses need to keep working as they grow.

intro.paragraph1: Deralis Digital is an independent engineering practice. I work with profitable, operational businesses where the way information moves between people and tools has become the bottleneck. Not websites. Not templates. Systems that show where work is, what is blocked, and what needs to happen next.
intro.paragraph2: Before Deralis, I worked inside equipment rental operations at Loxam and Ariane Systems. That experience produced TraviXO, a VGP compliance SaaS built specifically for the French equipment rental industry. That experience taught me one thing that shapes every engagement: you cannot design a system for an operation you do not understand.

philosophy.title: My philosophy
philosophy.subtitle: The system must survive without you.
philosophy.conclusion: Every system I build is transferable. Documented. Maintainable by another developer. You own the system. There is no lock-in.

whatWeBuild.title: What I build
whatWeBuild.description: I specialize in full-stack platform development.

saas.title: Operations management systems
saas.description: Web-based systems your team can access from anywhere, with logins, permissions, and recurring billing if you need it.

dashboards.title: Team dashboards
dashboards.description: One screen that shows the whole team where work is, what is blocked, and what needs attention. With proper logins and role-based visibility.

databases.title: Reliable data foundations
databases.description: The structured storage your operation needs, designed to grow with you and not lose data along the way.

automation.title: Process automation
automation.description: Connecting your existing tools so information moves between them without anyone copy-pasting. Triggers, scheduled tasks, integrations.

api.title: Tool connections
api.description: When two tools need to talk to each other but don't, I build the bridge.

whatWeBuild.note: Each system I build is designed for the specific operation it serves. No templates. No copy-pasted solutions.

howWeWork.title: How I work
howWeWork.description: Clients work directly with me, without layers, outsourcing, or miscommunication. The process is simple and technical:

step1.title: Discovery
step1.description: I sit with you and walk through how your operation actually works today, where time gets lost, and where the breakage hurts most.

step2.title: Design
step2.description: I sketch the system: what it does, what it shows, what happens when something goes wrong, how it grows with you.

step3.title: Build
step3.description: I write the code, set up the database, and build the interface. You see it as it takes shape, not at the end.

step4.title: Deploy
step4.description: I get it onto a live server, test it under real conditions, and walk you through how to use it.

step5.title: Support
step5.description: I am still around. Updates, fixes, additions, or a clean handover to another developer when you want one.

howWeWork.note: This workflow ensures stable delivery and reduces time-to-production.

techExpertise.title: Technology expertise
techExpertise.description: I use a modern, unified stack to keep development fast, predictable, and scalable.

screenshots.title: Real systems I have built
screenshots.description: Dashboards, workflows, and database systems used in production environments.

notes.note1: These systems include multi-tenant logic, role-based access, SQL databases, and automation workflows.
notes.note2: All platforms shown here are built with real databases, real logic, and real engineering, not templates.
notes.note3: This demonstrates my focus: systems, not pages.

principle.label: Principle
principle.text: No build begins without a shared definition of done. Getting there is the work that keeps you from paying for the wrong thing.

whyChoose.title: Why businesses choose Deralis Digital
items: You work directly with the person building it. No project manager, no junior handoff, no sales layer.
One person responsible for everything. The interface, the logic, the data, the deployment.
Built with current tools so the next developer who touches it can read it and continue.
whyChoose.conclusion: Deralis Digital is built for businesses that want their operation to run, not their software to impress.

location.title: Where I operate
location.description: Based in France. Working with clients worldwide.

cta.title: Want me to look at your operation?
cta.description: Before anything is quoted, I map how your system actually works.

metadata.title: About · Deralis Digital
metadata.description: Deralis Digital is an independent engineering practice. I build internal systems for profitable businesses where the way information moves between people and tools has become the bottleneck.

hero.title: About Deralis Digital.
hero.sub1: I build the systems businesses need to operate.
hero.sub2: Deralis Digital is an independent engineering practice. I work with profitable, operational businesses where the way information moves between people and tools has become the bottleneck. Not websites. Not templates. Systems that show where work is, what is blocked, and what needs to happen next.
hero.sub3: I’ve worked inside real businesses where the system isn’t visible, but the consequences are. That experience is what TraviXO Systems comes from, a VGP compliance software for the French equipment rental industry.
hero.sub4: It shapes how I work: you cannot design a system for an operation you do not understand.

background.title: Four years inside French software and industrial businesses.
background.intro: I work as a solo operator, building Deralis alongside my role in software support. My experience comes from working inside real systems where operations depend on tools that were never designed to work together.

role2.title: Helpdesk Technician
role2.company: Loxam · Technical support inside the French equipment rental industry

background.closing: The eighteen months at Loxam are where I learned how the French equipment rental industry actually operates day to day. That domain knowledge is what TraviXO Systems is built on.

principles.title: Three principles, no exceptions.
principles.intro: Every engagement runs through the same logic. Each principle exists because skipping it has cost someone, somewhere, money they did not need to spend.

p1.title: Audit first
p1.body: The €300 systems audit is the only way in. Nothing is built before that. The audit defines what to build and in what order, and the fee is deducted from the project if it proceeds.

p2.title: Define done before we start
p2.body: If we cannot write down what finished looks like, the project does not start. Scope without a definition of done is how engagements run forever and clients lose trust.

p3.title: I own the system. You own the business.
p3.body: Every system is documented, transferable, and maintainable by another developer. No lock-in. No hidden dependencies. You can take the build elsewhere the day it ships if that is what you need.

building.title: Two independent practices.
building.intro: Deralis Digital is the client practice. TraviXO Systems is the product.

card1.title: TraviXO Systems
card1.body: Live multi-tenant software for VGP compliance in the French equipment rental industry. Built solo from inside the industry it serves.

card2.title: Deralis Digital
card2.body: Independent engineering practice built around an audit-first model for operational businesses across France, the UK, and the US.

metadata.title: TraviXO Systems · Deralis Digital
metadata.description: A live multi-tenant VGP compliance SaaS for the French equipment rental industry. Built solo from domain expertise inside the sector.

travixo.title: A compliance system for the equipment rental industry.
travixo.standfirst: A live multi-tenant SaaS that manages VGP regulatory inspections for French equipment rental companies. Built solo, grounded in real industry constraints.

whatItIs.title: Compliance for the French equipment rental industry.
whatItIs.p1: TraviXO Systems is a multi-tenant compliance SaaS for the French equipment rental industry. It tracks the periodic regulatory inspections, the VGP, that every rental company in France is legally required to perform on the equipment they put in front of customers.
whatItIs.p2: Missed inspections are a liability problem and a regulatory problem, and the way most rental companies track them today is through a mix of paper folders, individual spreadsheets, and the memory of whoever has been there longest.
whatItIs.p3: The understanding behind it comes from time spent inside the equipment rental industry, close to how this work actually gets done. The product is shaped around those workflows rather than around a generic compliance template.
whatItIs.p4: Built solo. Live. Running real inspection workflows in production.

whatIBuilt.title: The pieces that make the system work.

1.title: Multi-tenant foundation
1.body: Each rental company operates in its own isolated workspace, with strict separation of data between tenants.

2.title: Equipment register
2.body: Every piece of rentable equipment is tracked with its identification, category, regulatory class, and inspection schedule. The register is the single source of truth that every other part of the system depends on.

3.title: Inspection workflow
3.body: Inspections are scheduled automatically based on regulatory frequency, surfaced as work to do, and recorded with the inspector identity, date, findings, and any required follow-up. Nothing is recorded on paper and forgotten.

4.title: Audit trail and exports
4.body: Every action is logged in a way that survives an audit by the regulator. Inspection records can be exported as compliance reports without anyone having to assemble a folder by hand the night before.

5.title: Role-aware access
5.body: The branch manager, the technician, and the head office accountant see different views of the same data. Each role sees what is theirs to act on, and only that.

inside.title: A few of the screens that run it.
inside.cap1: The equipment register, where every rentable item lives with its regulatory class and inspection schedule.
inside.cap2: The inspection workflow surfacing what is due, what is overdue, and what was completed in the last cycle.
inside.cap3: The exportable audit trail. Every action attributable, every record reproducible.

card1.title: Flux Client
card1.body: An intake-to-job-board demo showing how a single form can drive a real-time operational view for a small team.

card2.title: Onboard
card2.body: A staff and client onboarding tracker that replaces the spreadsheet most small teams quietly run their first weeks on.

card3.title: Government Application Portal
card3.body: A spec pitch for a structured application intake portal. Not delivered. Included here as scope reference.

metadata.title: Systems Audit · Deralis Digital
metadata.description: A paid decision point, not a conversation, not a report, and not a sales step. A structured 60 to 90 minute call plus a written decision brief, for 300 euros.

hero.headline: Find out what to build, and what not to build, before anyone writes code.
hero.sub1: The audit is a paid decision point, not a conversation, not a report, and not a sales step.
hero.sub2: One structured call, followed by a written decision brief delivered within five working days.
hero.sub3: Delivered by an independent systems engineer who builds live systems and works from real operational constraints.

walkaway.title: A set of decisions you can act on Monday morning.
walkaway.intro: The audit exists so you can stop guessing which system to fix first, and start executing against an order that holds up.
items: Decide which system to fix first, and why the others wait.
Decide whether to build, buy, or use what already exists.
Decide whether the problem is software or operations.
Decide what not to build yet, so the scope stops expanding on its own.

inPractice.disclaimerLead: Example scenario. Not a specific client.
inPractice.disclaimerBody: An illustrative composite, not a case study. No fabricated metrics, no client name, no implied track record.
inPractice.title: What an audit actually looks like.
inPractice.intro: The five pieces of a decision brief, walked through on a composite example.

1.heading: Situation
1.body: A French SME, about thirty staff, coordinating field operations across multiple sites. Profitable, growing, run day-to-day on WhatsApp, email, and three separate SaaS tools that didn’t talk to each other.

2.heading: What was breaking
2.body: Nobody could answer “where is this right now?” without making two phone calls. The same information was being entered into three places. The operations lead was spending a full day a week reconciling the gaps manually.

3.heading: What the audit clarified
3.body: The problem was not the tools. The problem was that no single place held the current state of a job. Adding a fourth tool would have made it worse. The fix was one thin layer on top of the existing tools, not a replacement of any of them.

4.heading: What the first build should be
4.body: A single status surface showing every active job, its current stage, and who owns the next step. Pulling from the existing tools, not replacing them. Scoped to ship in weeks, not months.

5.heading: What was explicitly ruled out
5.body: A full operations platform. A mobile app. A CRM replacement. Each of those was a real temptation the audit made it easier to say no to, because the brief named them and explained why they were wrong for this operation right now.

inPractice.midCtaNote: This is what the brief looks like. Same format, your operation, your decisions.

deliverables.title: Two things. A call, and a written brief.
deliverables.intro: No slide decks. No generic audit template. No recycled recommendations.
items: One structured audit call, 60 to 90 minutes.
A written decision brief, delivered after the call.
A friction map of how your operation actually runs today.
A what-to-build and what-not-to-build split, with reasoning.
An execution sequence and the first system scope direction.
excludes: No implementation work. The audit does not build anything.
No vague generic recommendations. The brief names your operation, not a category.

notFor.title: Don’t book the audit if…
notFor.intro: If any of these describe you, the audit is the wrong product. Save the €300.
items: You need a marketing website (WordPress, Webflow, Framer, and so on).
You want someone to start building before the problem is properly defined.
There is no operational friction to solve.
You cannot define what “better” would look like.
You expect to skip the audit and go straight to building.

refund.title: The audit is the work.
refund.p1: I don’t take money for audits I can’t deliver. If during the call I find I’m not the right person to help you, I refund the full €300. No arguments, no partial credit, no upsell into something else.
refund.p2: Otherwise the audit is non-refundable, because the audit is the work, not a sales step. The call and the brief exist so you can make decisions you couldn’t make before. That value is delivered whether or not you hire me afterwards, and that’s the point.

tailCta.headline: You are not buying a call. You are buying a decision.
tailCta.body: One fixed price, one structured call, one written decision brief. If I’m not the right person to help, you get your money back.
tailCta.note: Fixed price. Non-refundable unless I determine I’m not the right fit.

1.question: How long until I get the brief?
1.answer: Five working days from the audit call. If anything delays that, you hear from me before the deadline, not after.

2.question: What if I’m not ready to act on the brief?
2.answer: The brief is yours. Use it when the timing is right. There is no expiry, no follow-up sequence, no sales chase.

3.question: What if I want to hire you to build the first system after the audit?
3.answer: The €300 audit fee is deducted from the project cost if the project proceeds. The audit pays for itself the moment the build starts.

4.question: Do you sign NDAs?
4.answer: Yes. Standard mutual NDA, signed before the call if you want one. Send yours or I’ll send mine.

5.question: What happens to my data?
5.answer: It stays with me. Nothing is shared with third parties, nothing is used for marketing, nothing is stored longer than necessary to deliver the brief.

6.question: Can I share the brief with my team?
6.answer: Yes. The brief is yours to circulate, reference, and act on however you want. It’s written to be readable by someone who wasn’t on the call.

7.question: Can the call be in French?
7.answer: Yes. The call and the brief can be delivered in French or English. I’m bilingual and work daily in both.
